coredata.dc:title: Development of a green simple molecularly imprinted nanoprobe for rapid determination of trace thiamphenicol residual in animal derived foods
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shujuan
Ouyang, Wanlin
Zhu, Yiting
Han, Jing
Zhang, Yanan
Zou, Likou
Deng, Juan
Liu, Aiping
Liu, Shuliang
Yang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: An environmentally friendly nitrogen-sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe (N,S-CDs@MIP) was prepared for the detection of thiamphenicol (TAP) in animal-derived foods. The N,S-CDs, the core fluorophores in optical sensors, were synthesized from food waste of crayfish shells and l-cysteine and assembled to molecularly imprinted layers by Si–O–Si bond. The fluorescence produced by the novel probe in response to TAP ranged from 10.00 to 300.0 μg/L, with a detection limit of 6.20 μg/L. The developed method was also successfully applied to real milk, fish and tap water with 92.08–108.4% recoveries, which agrees with the high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis (89.15%–110.7%). The developed probe is cheap with a short detection time (30 min) and provides a theoretical and practical operation basis for monitoring TAP residue levels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thiamphenicol
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Fluorescent probe
Carbon quantum dots
Rapid detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N,S-CDs@MIPs and N,S-CDs@NIPs nanocomposites
Linear relationship between TAP concentration and FL intensity of N,S-CDs@MIPs（N,S-CDs@NIPs）
Possible fluorescence sensing mechanism of N,S-CDs@MIPs
Determination of TAP in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of N, S‒CDs@MIPs and the sensing process of thiamphenicol detection.
TEM images of (a) N,S‒CDs, (b) the size distribution of the N,S‒CDs; the inset of (a): the HRTEM imaging of the N,S‒CDs; SEM images of (c) N,S‒CDs@MIPs and (d) N,S‒CDs@NIPs.
XRD pattern of N,S‒CDs@MIPs (a) and FT‒IR spectra of N,S‒CDs and N,S‒CDs@MIPs.

(a) FL emission spectra of N,S‒CDs@MIPs with the addition of the indicated concentrations of TAP. (b) Corresponding FL intensity Stern‒Volmer plots of N,S‒CDs@MIPs: y = 0.001x+0.5481, R2 = 0.9956. (c) FL emission spectra of N,S‒CDs@NIPs with the addition of the indicated concentrations of TAP. (d) Corresponding FL intensity Stern‒Volmer plots of N,S‒CDs@NIPs: y = 0.0008x+0.4055, R2 = 0.9279. (The concentrations of TAP were 0, 10, 20, 40, 50, 80,100, 150, 200, and 300 mg/L).

(a) Selectivity of the N,S‒CDs@MIPs and N,S‒CDs@NIPs over different kinds of antibiotics (TAP, FF, CAP, PG, DOX, SD, CEX, AMX and MEL in the concentration of 100 μg/L). Numbers on the histograms represent IF of each antibiotic. (b) Quenching efficiency responses of N,S‒CDs@MIPs to increases in the CCAP/CTAP ratio.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S‒CDs@MIPs, TAP, the mixture of N,S‒CDs@MIPs and TAP; (b) the synchronous fluorescence spectrum (446 nm) of N,S‒CDs@MIPs and the UV absorption spectrum of TAP; (c) Fluorescence decay curves of pure N,S‒CDs@MIPs and N,S‒CDs@MIPs after interacting with TAP; (d) Temperature‒dependent response of F0–F/F0 to N,S‒CDs@MIPs + TAP.
Effect of interfering substances of N,S‒CDs@MIPs‒TAP solution system.
Determination of TAP in spiked real samples by N,S‒CDs@MIPs.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of TAP.

row.entry: Purification and separation by organic solvents
Purification and reflux of metal mixture
Prepare and screen the polyclonal antibodies

head.ce:title: Development of a green simple molecularly imprinted nanoprobe for rapid determination of trace thiamphenicol residual in animal derived foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya'an, Sichuan, 625014, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An environmentally friendly nitrogen-sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe (N,S-CDs@MIP) was prepared for the detection of thiamphenicol (TAP) in animal-derived foods. The N,S-CDs, the core fluorophores in optical sensors, were synthesized from food waste of crayfish shells and l-cysteine and assembled to molecularly imprinted layers by Si–O–Si bond. The fluorescence produced by the novel probe in response to TAP ranged from 10.00 to 300.0 μg/L, with a detection limit of 6.20 μg/L. The developed method was also successfully applied to real milk, fish and tap water with 92.08–108.4% recoveries, which agrees with the high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis (89.15%–110.7%). The developed probe is cheap with a short detection time (30 min) and provides a theoretical and practical operation basis for monitoring TAP residue levels.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiamphenicol (TAP), a structural analog of chloramphenicol, is a novel and effective broad-spectrum antibiotic drug (Li et al., 2020), widely used in treating bacterial infectious diseases in edible animals. However, TAP residues in feeds, milk, and aquatic products promote the evolution of drug-resistant bacteria, resulting in embryotoxicity, hematotoxicity and a serious threat to human health (Hu et al., 2016). To control it, the European Union (EU) and the Ministry of Agriculture of China have set the maximum residue limits (MRL) of TAP in feeds at 50.0 μg/kg (Liu et al., 2018). Given the seriousness of this regulation, rapid and efficient detection methods for TAP have received extensive attention.

At present, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Xie et al., 2011) coupled to mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Wang et al., 2019) and gas chromatography coupled to an MS (GC-MS) (Nagata & Oka, 1996) are the most common and effective methods for detecting TAP. However, these chromatographic assays need a long analysis process and involve toxic reagent pollution. In addition, the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is expensive and prone to cross-reactions, affecting the assay results (Li et al., 2018). Therefore, creating a simple and effective new technique for TAP testing is of great interest to avoid the shortcomings of existing analytical methods.

Recently, optosensor based on fluorescence spectroscopy has been widely used in food analytical fields because it is cost-effective, simple and rapid (Pan et al., 2019). Several other sensors have been developed, including CdSe, ZnS, PbS, CdTe QDs, semiconductor quantum dots (Chan et al., 2002) and carbon quantum dots (CDs). Despite their low quantum yield (QY) and stability, the CDs avoid the risk of heavy metal pollution and costly defects and have low toxicity, good water solubility, and more abundant raw material resources (Luo et al., 2020). Heteroatomic doping is one of the most effective strategies to regulate the photoluminescence performance and quantum yield of CDs. Dong et al.(Dong et al., 2013) proved that each dopant would have different effects on the fluorescence mechanism of CDs. For example, N,S-CDs have superior fluorescence (FL), QY and FL lifetime than CDs and N-CDs, providing an important scientific reference for the research on their practical application. However, the selectivity and detection accuracy of CDs need to be improved to detect trace substances in complex food matrix (Liu, Ding, et al., 2019). Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) can also be integrated into mixed sensing materials as highly specific receptors (Chullasat et al., 2018) since they can selectively bind target compounds in complex substrates for clinical diagnosis, environmental control, food analysis and drug screening (Liu, Huang, et al., 2019). But the major obstacle is how to efficiently combine MIP and CDs and simultaneously reduce the aggregation and FL loss of CDs as much as possible. Therefore, CDs nanoparticles can be chemically modified effectively by multifunctional organosilane‒APTES (3‒aminopropyl triethoxysilane). The surface of CDs can also be effectively combined with the silanol group to form the Si–O–Si bond, which promotes the effective expansion of chemical functional groups on the molecular surface, and facilitates the flexible assembly of CDs to MIPs. The formed cross-linked silica shell layer also effectively prevents the electrostatic agglomeration and extravasation of CD nanoparticles by reducing the interference of the fluorescence properties of CDs to a minimum (Chemtob et al., 2014). Currently, there are few studies on fluorescent sensors assembled by CDs and MIPs via multifunctional organosilanes for recognition of zearalenone (Shao et al., 2018)，indoxacarb (Shirani et al., 2021) and dopamine (Mao et al., 2012).

These studies synthesized the core fluorescent nanomaterials with newer and more novel ideas using carbon-rich kitchen waste as carbon precursors. Alternatively, the waste crayfish shells commonly found on Chinese dining tables are rich in protein and chitin, which meet the carbon source requirements of CDs and can be combined with the co-doping of N and S elements to develop sensors (Fan et al., 2020). Besides, these studies lack a more comprehensive and optimized preparation and detection mechanism. Due to the complex composition of the food matrix, this green and efficient technique has also not been used to detect TAP residues in animal-derived foods.

In our research, a novel nitrogen-sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe (N,S-CDs@MIPs) was fabricated by a simple sol-gel polymerization method to test and detect TAP residues in food samples. We used crayfish shells and L-Cysteine as carbon precursors and dopants to prepare the environmentally friendly CDs by hydrothermal method without any toxic solvents. We efficiently immobilized the CDs and flexibly assembled them to MIPs via APTES. The developed N,S-CDs@MIPs were practically more effective in detecting TAP in milk, fish and tap water than other methods in time, detection limits and recovery.

The antibiotics, including TAP, florfenicol (FF), chloramphenicol (CAP), amoxicillin (AMX), sulfonamide (SDI), penicillin (PG), doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX), and melamine (MEL) were sourced from Chengdu Kelon Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and l-cysteine were purchased from the Shanghai Yuanye BioTechnology Co., Ltd. Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and the functional monomer 3‒aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) were provided by Chengdu Huaxia Reagent Co., Ltd, while Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer solutions were from H3PO4 (phosphoric acid) –HAC (acetic acid) –H3BO3 (boric acid) mixed‒acid solution. All the chemicals mentioned above were of analytical grade (≥98%). Crayfish, milk and fish samples were obtained from the Ya'an Farmers Market.

The morphologies and size of N,S-CDs were visualized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL‒2100F, JEOL, Japan), and the structure of MIPs coated N,S-CDs recorded on scanning electron microscopy (SEM, LEO-1530VP, Zeiss, Germany). To identify functional groups, the infrared spectra at 4000-400 cm−1 with the KBr pellet technique were registered on a NICOLEF IS 10 FTIR apparatus (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. Ltd. USA). An XRD pattern of the N,S-CDs and MIPs-coated N,S-CDs were performed on a D/Max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The XPS spectra were recorded on an ESCALAB250 electron spectrometer (Thermo Electron, USA). The PL and UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a Varioskan Flashmultimode reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co.). The Abs-QY of N,S-CDs@MIPs were measured as described previously (Liu et al., 2016) through a calibrated integrating sphere (IS) in the FLSP920 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd. Scotland, UK). The nanosecond fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out on an Edinburgh FLSP920 time-corrected single photon counting system. All fluorescence detection was conducted on a Varioskan Flash multimode reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co.). The standard control test of food samples was investigated by an LC‒10A2010C HT Liquid Chromatograph (Shimadzu).

The N,S-CDs were prepared using a modified method described previously (Zhao et al., 2020). Briefly, 2.0000 g of crayfish shells were powdered, mixed with 1.0000 g of L-Cysteine, topped up to 100.0 mL ultrapure water and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The brownish mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min to remove the residue and the supernatants collected by filtration using 0.22 μm filter papers before dialysis (MW, 1000 Da) for 12 h to purify the N,S-CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N,S-CDs@MIPs and N,S-CDs@NIPs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: We modified the surface of N,S-CDs to bind well to molecular imprinting by adding 2.00 mL of APTES in droplets to 20.0 mL of N,S-CDs (1.75 mg/mL). The solution was then stirred at 60 °C for 12 h, and the modified N,S-CDs were extracted twice with diethyl ether to remove excess reagents.

The N,S-CDs@MIPs were fabricated using a sol-gel method previously described (Mao et al., 2012) with a few modifications. Briefly, 20.0 mg of TAP was dissolved in 20.0 mL of the modified N,S-CDs solution, using ultrasonic vibration for 10 min to dissolve TAP completely. The solution was then mixed with 200 μL of APTES, 1.00 mL of TEOS and 100 μL of NH3·H2O and stirred for 30 min, followed by adding 0.80 mL of 0.40 mol/L CTAB and 0.10 mL of 0.20 mol/L NaOH and continuous stirring for 12 h at room temperature. The synthesized molecularly imprinted polymer was repeatedly washed with methanol and water (mixed in the ratio of 4:1, v/v) to remove TAP and CTAB, with no TAP detection by UV spectroscopy at 225 nm, indicating complete removal. A non-imprinted polymer-coated N,S-CDs (N,S-CDs@NIPs) were similarly prepared but without TAP to serve as the control.

All the optical properties of N,S-CDs@MIPs and N,S-CDs@NIPs were evaluated with fluorescent spectra ranging from 400 to 600 nm and an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The N,S-CDs@MIP solution was diluted after the removal of template molecules to generate test solutions. A standard solution of 10.00–300.0 μg/L of TAP was thoroughly mixed with 0.20 mg/mL N,S-CDs@MIPs and BR buffer solutions (pH 8.0) (2:2:1, v/v/v) through ultrasonic treatment. The solution mixture was then incubated for 40 min at room temperature in a shaker, and the fluorescence was measured using a spectrophotometer. All fluorescence measurements were performed at 360 nm excitation wavelength and 480 nm emission wavelength with a slit width of 10 nm, and the spectra were analyzed after three parallel scans.

Fish, milk and tap water were pretreated according to previously described methods (Chen et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2019), while the controlled trial was performed using HPLC-UV. The details are included in Supplementary Material.

The N,S-CDs or fluorescent signal transducers were synthesized from crayfish shell food waste and modified by adding TAP, APTES, crosslinker TEOS, structure-oriented surfactant CTAB and catalyst NH3H2O (Fig. 1 ) and polymerized by sol-gel method to form a MIPs-coated N,S-CDs imprinted layer. Amino-bearing APTES and TAP molecules bind through weak interactions such as hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces, allowing them to be placed in a polymer matrix (Yang et al., 2019). Therefore, after removing CTAB and TAP, the obtained N,S-CDs@MIP possesses a mesoporous structure and a specific recognition site cavity complementary to TAP. The photoluminescence intensity of N,S-CDs@MIPs can also be recovered, and its imprinted cavity can selectively recognize TAPs in complex food samples.

Because reusability is important in fluorescent probes, we used methanol to elute TAP in N,S-CDs@MIPs(Fig. S1). In order to ensure the uniformity of the test method, the repetitive experiments of N,S-CDs@NIPs were also conducted under the same conditions. After five cycles, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs@MIPs and N,S-CDs@NIPs decreased slightly, proving the good reusability of the sensor.

The N,S-CDs had an average diameter size of 2.38 ± 0.14 nm, and clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm (Fig. 2 a), attributed to the (100) plane of the graphitic carbon framework diffraction (Zhang et al., 2019). The N,S-CDs@MIPs and N,S-CDs@NIPs also had regular spherical polymer particles with an average size (240–260 nm) larger than that of N,S-CDs (Fig. 2c and d). The fluorescent nanomaterials were also successfully encapsulated by the imprinted layer.

Both N,S-CDs@MIPs and N,S-CDs had similar XRD patterns (Fig. 3 a). They formed a typical broad peak at about 23.6–24.0°, corresponding to the 002 planes of the graphitic carbon structure, indicating that N,S-CD holds an amorphous carbon structure (Atchudan et al., 2016). This is closely related to the fact that the functional groups such as N, S, oxygen and silicon were introduced on the surface of N,S-CDs@MIPs. This confirms that the single probe of N,S-CDs successfully formed the central fluorescent material of the composite probe of N,S-CDs@MIPs. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3b) showed the functional group structures of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs@MIPs, with two characteristic peaks at 3424 and 2940 cm−1, which can be assigned to the vibrations of –OH and –NH groups and carbon-hydrogen bond, respectively (Mao et al., 2012). The synthesized N,S-CDs exhibited adsorption peaks at 1720, 1659 and 1416 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CO, N–H and C–O–C groups, respectively (Liao et al., 2018). The infrared spectra of N,S-CDs@MIPs showed characteristic peaks at 1608 and 1384 cm−1, confirming the existence of APTES and TEOS by the bending vibrations of N–H and C–H (Gupta et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2009). The characteristic peak at 1066 cm−1 can be ascribed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of Si–O–Si, and 794 cm−1 to symmetric stretching vibrations of Si–O–Si and bending vibrations of Si–O bonds (Wu et al., 2017) of N,S-CDs@MIPs. The bonds described above further confirmed that the imprinted layer produced through the sol-gel condensation method and induced by the silane reagent was successfully attached to the surface of N,S-CDs.

The four typical peaks of N,S-CDs at 170.1, 289.9, 400.8 and 535.2 eV (Fig. S2a) correspond to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s (Zhao et al., 2020) with elemental ratios of 0.36, 77.19, 8.59 and 13.86%. The four states of carbon atoms were CC/C–C (284.4 eV), C–N/C–S (285.3 eV), CO (286.6 eV) and CN/NO (288.2 eV), respectively (Gu et al., 2018). The XPS spectrum of N1s exhibited three peaks at N-(H)3 (399.5 eV), OC–N (400.3 eV) (Shi et al., 2017) and N–H (401.2 eV) (Fig. S2c). The CO and C–O–C/C–OH functional groups (Liao et al., 2018) were observed at 531.3 and 532.8 eV, respectively (Fig. S2c and Fig. S2d). The high-resolution spectrum of the S element can be divided into three main peaks (Fig. S2e), with those at 163.5 and 164.5 eV attributed to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 of C–S and the one at 168.1 eV, induced by sulfate or sulfonate (-C–SO3–) (Chen et al., 2016). These results demonstrate the graphitic nature of the structure of CDs and verify the successful doping of N,S-CDs.

The N,S-CDs@MIPs showed five peaks at 290.9, 403.1, 538.9, 108.6 and 170.4 eV in the full-range XPS spectral analysis, and were attributed to Cls, Nls, Ols, Si2p, and S1s, with elemental ratios of 56.38, 8.66, 23.84, 10.81 and 0.31%, respectively (Fig. S3a). The data indicated the presence of Si element in N,S-CDs@MIPs, template molecules and structural activators in the eluted polymer (Liu, Ding, et al., 2019). Two single peaks of CC/C–C (284.8 eV) and C–N (285.9 eV) were observed in the spectra of C1s in N,S-CDs@MIPs (Fig. S3b), which is consistent with the characterization of infrared functional groups. The spectra of N1s in N,S-CDs@MIPs (Fig. S3c) show that N mainly exists in the form of N–H and C–N–C, while the O1s in N,S-CDs@MIPs (Fig. S3d) exist in the form of CO and C–OH. Compared with the XPS spectra of N,S-CDs, N,S-CDs@MIPs significantly increased the characteristic peaks of Si, further confirming the successful synthesis of the MIPs-coated N,S-CDs from the sol-gel method (Yang et al., 2019).

The synthesis of N,S-CDs@MIPs can be affected by many factors (Wang et al., 2009). For example, electrostatic force or hydrogen bond interactions can bind the template molecules and functional monomers, which control the imprinted polymers. The degree of pre-polymerization directly affects the adsorption and recognition ability of imprinted polymers, while the amount of crosslinking agent directly affects the thickness of the imprinted shell, the fluorescence intensity of the probe, and the accessibility of the recognition site and the adsorption capacity of the polymer. In polymerization, N,S-CDs act as the core support carrier, and the imprinted shell grows gradually with it as the core under the action of the crosslinking agent. Thus, adding N,S-CDs affects the particle size and dispersion of molecularly imprinted fluorescent probes. In our study, the optimal synthesis conditions for N,S-CDs@MIPs consisted of 70.0 mg, 100 μL, 40.0 mL, and 500 μL of TAP, APTES, N,S-CDs and TEOS, respectively (Fig. S4). The optimized response conditions of N,S-CDs@MIPs to TAP (Fig. S5) included a 200-fold dilution of the N,S-CDs@MIPs, pH 7.5, system temperature at 25 °C and 30 min response time.

To further verify the identification and mass transfer efficiency of TAP by N,S-CDs@MIPs, we conducted experiments on the kinetic adsorption and adsorption equilibrium of N,S-CDs@MIPs and N,S-CDs@NIPs as described previously (Chen et al., 2021). The concentration of TAP was 100 μg/L, and the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs@MIPs and N,S-CDs@NIPs was measured every 10 min for 2 h, using the F0/F as the standard to judge the adsorption rate (Fig. S6a). The kinetic curves of N,S-CDs@MIPs increased rapidly with time in the first 30 min and benefited from the sufficient imprinted cavities in the initial stage of adsorption and the small resistance to mass transfer. The adsorption equilibration time was about 30 min, confirming the fluorescence response time of the previous study. Compared with N,S-CDs@NIPs, N,S-CDs@MIPs exhibited higher F0/F, better binding capacity and faster mass transfer rate. The adsorption equilibrium performed on the N,S-CDs@MIPs and N,S-CDs@NIPs with TAP showed an increase in both the adsorption capacities of the probes and controls as the TAP concentration increased (Fig. S6b). However, higher TAP concentrations made TAP occupy most of the recognition sites, resulting in slow changes in F0/F and reduced adsorption amount before reaching the adsorption equilibrium. The N,S-CDs@NIPs had no recognition sites, making them rely mainly on the non-specific sites of surface to adsorb, with little change in F0/F. These results show that the abundant and specific cavities (Li et al., 2016) on the surface of N,S-CDs@MIPs, ensured the selective recognition of TAPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear relationship between TAP concentration and FL intensity of N,S-CDs@MIPs（N,S-CDs@NIPs）
ce:section.ce:para:
The FL intensity of N,S-CDs@MIPs decreased significantly with the increased TAP concentration (Fig. 4 a). However, the FL quenching effect of N,S-CDs@NIPs was poor under the same conditions (Fig. 4c) due to lack of specific recognition sites, making TAP to only contact the N,S-CDs located on their surface but not in them. The N,S-CDs@MIP probes showed good linearity in TAP concentrations ranging from 10.00 to 300.0 μg/L following a series of optimized conditions, with a linear equation of F0–F/F0 = 0.001CTAP +0.5481 (R
2 = 0.9956) and a limit of detection of (3δ/K) 6.20 μg/L (Fig. 4b). The linear equation of N,S-CDs@NIPs was (F0–F/F0) = 0.0008CTAP +0.4055 (R 2 = 0.9279) (Fig. 4d). Where F0 was the initial fluorescence intensity of the detection system, and F the fluorescence intensity recorded after adding TAP to the detection system. We also found that TAP forms specific imprinting sites on the surface of N,S-CDs@MIPs, enabling it to be timely and effectively adsorbed and significantly improving the selectivity and sensitivity of the spectral technology. The technology can also produce an accurate response effect to TAP in food and ensure reliable sensitivity.

We performed interference and selectivity experiments to investigate whether some biomolecules and metal ions (Chen et al., 2019) commonly found in food matrices significantly affect the accuracy of TAP detection by N,S-CDs@MIPs. Our data showed little changes in the FL intensity of the N,S-CDs@MIPs-TAP detection system under various interference factors, such as metal ions and amino acids at high concentrations of 500.0–5000 mg/L, with a relative error of less than 4% (Table 1 ).

We selected antibiotics, including analogs of TAP and several biological agents in the food matrix, for selective experiments, with TAP showing the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency. TAP also had the highest imprinting factor (IF) of 3.05 among the antibiotics with concentrations of 100.0 μg/L (Fig. 5 a), indicating that N,S-CDs@MIPs possess the highest selectivity and recognition ability for TAP. Afterwards, we selected CAP, a structural analog of TAP, and tested it as an increasing competing antibiotic with TAP as a constant concentration in the quenching efficiency of N,S-CDs@MIPs + TAP + CAP (Fig. 5b). However, the quenching efficiency of N,S-CDs@MIPs was not significantly affected by the increased CCAP/CTAP ratio. This can be attributed to the formation of imprinted cavities in the imprinted shell, complementary to TAP in size and shape during the preparation of N,S-CDs@MIP probes, causing tight binding of TAP to N,S-CDs@MIPs and significant quenching effect, unlike the inconsistent spatial configuration of CAP with cavities left by TAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence sensing mechanism of N,S-CDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption spectra of N,S-CDs@MIPs, TAP, and the mixture of N,S-CDs@MIPs and TAP are shown in Fig. 6 a. We observed an additional absorption peak at 300 nm after mixing N,S-CDs@MIPs with TAP, inferring the formation of a new non-luminescent complex by π-π superposition between fluorescent and quenched molecules. The data also do not attribute the binding of TAP on the surface of N,S-CDs@MIPs to physisorption (Yang et al., 2018). There is also no overlap between the UV absorption peak of TAP and the emission peak of N,S-CDs@MIPs in the overall wavelength segment, indicating that TAP does not absorb the fluorescence emission of N,S-CDs@MIPs (Fig. 6b). This was inconsistent with the internal filtering effect (IFE) in the quenching mechanism, suggesting the exclusion of IFE and incorporation of the interaction between the two molecular systems (Hu et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). We, therefore, hypothesize that the hydroxyl or halogen atoms of TAP form hydrogen-bonded ground-state complexes with the N-hydrogen bonds of N,S-CDs@MIPs, causing them to aggregate and reduce their π-π* excitation, and eventually leads to fluorescence quenching.

The fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs@MIPs was 1.72 ns and 1.58 ns after adding TAP (Fig. 6c). Because the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) can produce obvious fluorescence lifetime changes, the quenching mechanism of FRET can be excluded (Liu et al., 2017). The excited state information of molecules can be judged by the fluorescence lifetime. The collision between the excited state fluorophore and the dynamic quench will significantly shorten the fluorescence lifetime, while the static quench has the opposite effect (Hu et al., 2019). The use of the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (KSV) to judge the basis for the quenching mechanism showed an increasing temperature of the detection system with the decreasing KSV (Fig. 6d), which is consistent with the static quenching process (Tsai et al., 2019). Therefore, based on the fluorescence lifetime and theoretical analysis, the quenching mechanism of TAP and N,S-CDs@MIPs is static.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TAP in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, we designed a novel hybrid fluorescent sensing probe for TAP detection using environmentally friendly biomass carbon quantum dots (N,S-CDs) and molecularly imprinted layers. The sensing probe had a satisfactory low LOD of 6.20 μg/L), recoveries of 92.08%–108.4% and a shorter detection time of 30 min in the analysis of complex food samples. Compared with traditional analytical techniques, recycling food waste as a raw material solves the problems of high cost and toxicity. The sensor also combines blotting technology and fluorescence detection, giving it the dual advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity. However, the experiment had a single referenced template molecule, which can be expanded using existing studies. Other food samples also contain different interference substances, which could be included to determine detection limits. With the rapid development of various signaling units such as multifunctional MIPs and CDs, we also speculate that the synergistic effect will provide great prospects for exploring the potential applications of composite materials.

Shujuan Chen: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Wanlin Ouyang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yiting Zhu: Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Jing Han: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yanan Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Likou Zou: Writing – review & editing. Juan Deng: Visualization. Aiping Liu: Writing – review & editing. Shuliang Liu: Supervision. Yong Yang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Department of Sichuan Province (2023YFG0231, 2022ZDZX0017, 2020ZHCG0044, 2021YFH0192), Science and Technology Bureau of Chengdu [2022‒YF05‒00487‒SN].

Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, J., Lin, Z.-Z., Nur, A. Z., Lu, Y., Wu, M.-H., Zeng, J., Chen, X.-M., & Huang, Z.-Y. (2018). Detection of trace tetracycline in fish via synchronous fluorescence quenching with carbon quantum dots coated with molecularly imprinted silica. Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy, 190, 450-456. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.09.066

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yang et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorometric determination of fipronil by integrating the advantages of molecularly imprinted silica and carbon quantum dots
Microchimica Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, C., Wang, L., Zhang, Z., Chen, Y., Deng, Q., & Wang, S. (2019). Fluorometric determination of fipronil by integrating the advantages of molecularly imprinted silica and carbon quantum dots. Microchimica Acta, 187(1), 12. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604-019-4005-5

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fish-scale-derived carbon dots as efficient fluorescent nanoprobes for detection of ferric ions
RSC Advances

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Y., Gao, Z., Yang, X., Chang, J., Liu, Z., & Jiang, K. (2019). Fish-scale-derived carbon dots as efficient fluorescent nanoprobes for detection of ferric ions. RSC Advances, 9(2), 940-949. https://doi.org/10.1039/C8RA09471C

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhao et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent nanoprobe for determination of tetracyclines and cell imaging
Microchimica Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, N., Wang, Y., Hou, S., & Zhao, L. (2020). Functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent nanoprobe for determination of tetracyclines and cell imaging. Microchimica Acta, 187(6), 351. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604-020-04328-1
